items.front: Under 2-3-1, what is the procedure for correcting altitude information on a flight progress strip?
items.back: Do NOT erase or overwrite. Use 'X' to delete unwanted altitude info/symbols. Write new altitude immediately adjacent within the same space. Draw a horizontal line through vacated altitude only AFTER aircraft reports/is observed leaving it. Preplanning may be done in red pencil.

items.front: Under 2-3-1, when is a slant line through zero required? When is it optional?
items.back: REQUIRED: on all weather data. OPTIONAL (but use when misunderstanding possible): on handwritten portions of flight progress strips. Same rule applies to underline of letter 's'.

items.front: Under 2-3-1c, how may altitude information be abbreviated on strips?
items.back: In thousands of feet (e.g., 5,000 as '5', 2,800 as '2.8', FL330 as '33') — only when authorized by the facility manager and defined in a facility directive.

items.front: Under 2-3-2a Block 20a, when is RA event recording REQUIRED vs OPTIONAL?
items.back: OPTIONAL: when voice recorders are operational. REQUIRED: when voice recorders are NOT operating and strips are being used. Format: 'RA' followed by climb/descent arrow (if reported) and time (hhmm).

items.front: Under 2-3-2c, which blocks can facility air traffic managers repurpose for point out, radar vector, speed adjustment, or transfer of control data?
items.back: Blocks 13, 14, 14a, 22, 23, 24, and 28 (seven blocks).

items.front: Under 2-3-2a Block 26, what information is recorded here?
items.back: Pertinent remarks, minimum fuel, point out/radar vector/speed adjustment information, or sector/position number (per 2-2-1), or NRP.

items.front: Under 2-3-3a, list the four reduced separation flags available in ATOP Block 4.
items.back: M — Mach Number Technique (MNT). R — Reduced MNT. D or 3 — Distance-based longitudinal (50 NM or 30 NM). W — Reduced Vertical Separation Minimum (RVSM). Selectable for aircraft whose flight plans contain required equipment qualifiers.

items.front: Under 2-3-3a, what is the order of preference for reported flight level display in ATOP Block 7?
items.back: 1. Mode C, 2. ADS, 3. Position reports. Displayed in that order.

items.front: Under 2-3-4d, what five conditions must be met for a terminal facility to waive flight progress strip usage?
items.back: 1. Backup systems (multiple radar sites/systems) utilized. 2. Local procedures documented in facility directive (departure, arrival, overflight, nonradar transition, ESL transition). 3. No misunderstanding from no-strip usage. 4. Unused strips/forms/notepads provided. 5. Revert to strips if backup unavailable.

items.front: Under 2-3-4, list the numbered blocks of the terminal flight progress strip.
items.back: Block 1: ACID. Block 2: CID. Block 3: number/type/equip. Block 4: CID if req'd. Block 5: beacon code. Block 6: coord fix. Block 7: overflight coord indicator (FDIO only). Block 8: ETA at coord fix. Block 8A: RA events (required when no voice recorder). Block 9: altitude/route. Blocks 9A-9C: optional. Blocks 10-18: per facility directive.

items.front: Under 2-3-5, what is the maximum character count for aircraft identity on strips?
items.back: Seven alphanumeric characters, not to exceed this for any type.

items.front: Under 2-3-5b, how are military aircraft identities formatted on strips?
items.back: Branch/mission prefix + last 5 digits of serial number (last 4 for CFC/CTG). Or pronounceable word (3-6 letters) + 1-4 digit number. Or assigned double-letter 2-digit flight number. Navy/Marine fleet: prefix + 2 letters (phonetic) + 2-3 digits, or prefix + digit + letter (phonetic) + 2-3 digits.

items.front: Under 2-3-5 TBL 2-3-8, what are the aircraft identifiers for the President?
items.back: AF1 (Air Force), VM1 (Marine), VV1 (Navy), RR1 (Army), C1 (Coast Guard), G1 (Guard), EXEC1 (Commercial). Family uses EXEC1F for all services.

items.front: Under 2-3-5a, what special identifiers use 'T' and 'L' letters in civil aircraft identity?
items.back: 'T' for air taxi (e.g., TN5552Q). 'L' for MEDEVAC (e.g., LN751B). Note: 'L' is NOT to be used for air carrier/air taxi MEDEVAC aircraft.

items.front: Under 2-3-6, where are approved aircraft type designators found?
items.back: FAA Order 7360.1, Aircraft Type Designators.

items.front: Under 2-3-7, what does the 'Z' suffix indicate and where is it used vs. not used?
items.back: USAF/USN undergraduate student pilots (restricted to VFR flight). Used: in flight plan aircraft identification. NOT used: in ground-to-air communication.

items.front: Under 2-3-7, what cloud penetration is allowed for USAF solo students with instrument certification?
items.back: May penetrate cloud layers in climb or descent ONLY. Cannot penetrate clouds in level flight. Revised clearance requests to avoid clouds in level flight can still be expected.

items.front: Under 2-3-8a, how do you change an aircraft equipment suffix?
items.back: Modify the CNS equipment codes in the ICAO flight plan; automation translates them into the proper suffix. Do NOT directly change the suffix with a symbol preceded by a slant — this may unintentionally alter or delete other equipment codes.

items.front: Under 2-3-8, what are the four GNSS equipment suffixes and how can GNSS be verified?
items.back: /G, /L, /S, or /V. Verified by: 1. ICAO flight plan readout showing 'G' in equipment list. 2. Verifying with the pilot that the aircraft is GNSS-equipped.

items.front: Under 2-3-8, which suffixes are for ATC use only and not in the AIM?
items.back: /H (failed transponder) and /O (failed Mode C).

items.front: Under 2-3-9, contrast the three clearance limit delay symbols: H, F, and O.
items.back: H: holding included in original clearance; detailed holding info follows dash if differs from established pattern. F and O: clearance limit set when delay is NOT anticipated — no holding expected.

items.front: Under 2-3-10 TBL 2-3-11, match clearance abbreviations: A, C, CAF, D, F, H, O, PD, T, V, X, Z.
items.back: A=Cleared to airport, C=ATC clears (relay), CAF=Cleared as filed, D=Cleared to depart from fix, F=Cleared to fix, H=Cleared to hold, O=Cleared to outer marker, PD=Pilot's discretion climb/descend, T=Cleared through, V=Cleared over fix, X=Cleared to cross at point, Z=Tower jurisdiction.

items.front: Under 2-3-10, what do the abbreviations RP and RX mean?
items.back: RP: Report immediately upon passing (fix/altitude). RX: Report crossing.

items.front: Under 2-3-10b, where can locally approved identifiers be used and NOT be used?
items.back: Used: only within your facility. NOT used: on teletypewriter or interphone circuits.
